chapter.title: Quickstart
chapter.simpara: The following sections will bring you through the successive steps to create your first "Hello World" scenario.

Before you get further, ensure you have installed Java 11 or above SDK. While you can develop a complete scenario with a text editor, we encourage you to use a suitable IDE such as IntelliJ or Eclipse.

section.title: Create a project with Gradle - Kotlin DSL
section.simpara: Create a new project for Gradle with your IDE, using the Kotlin DSL.

callout.para: The executable file is packaged as a "fat JAR" using the shadow plugin, containing all the dependencies required to run QALIPSIS and your scenario. The file is bigger but portable.
Update the description to customize your project
Dependencies required to develop scenarios with Kotlin
Dependencies required to develop scenarios for QALIPSIS, whatever the language is
When verifying the data in a scenario, you might like to use a test or assertion library you are familiar with, feel free to use JUnit, AssertJ or AssertK, Hamcrest or any other.
Dependencies to execute QALIPSIS, either as a head or factory.
Dependencies to perform the annotation processing at compile time. It is very important to prepare the later execution.
Default configuration or the application, if you ever want to test it locally. This is optional, but often helpful.
When the "fat JAR" will be packaged, we want to add a classifier on the artefact to distinguish it from the "normal JAR".
Include the creation of the "fat JAR" in the standard build workflow.

note.simpara: If you are using a snapshot version of QALIPSIS, add https://oss.sonatype.org/content/repositories/snapshots as a dependency repository.

section.title: Setting up your IDE
section.simpara: QALIPSIS relies on annotation processing at compile time to prepare the executable code and list the scenarios to later execute.
Furthermore, QALIPSIS relies on Micronaut IoC framework, which requires the following setup.

section.title: IntelliJ
section.simpara: Please follow the instructions provided by Micronaut’s team in section for IntelliJ.

section.title: Eclipse
section.simpara: Please follow the instructions provided by Micronaut’s team in section for Eclipse.

section.title: Visual studio code
section.simpara: Please follow the instructions provided by Micronaut’s team in section for Visual Studio Code.

section.title: Create a scenario
section.simpara: in the following sections, we will learn how to create a scenario specifications that simulates minions starting with a ramp-up, write a message on the console and stop.
Scenarios can be created in classes or Kotlin objects or as a Kotlin function. Names do not matter, the only important thing is to annotate the method:

important.simpara: Due to the incremental compilation not yet fully taken into account in QALIPSIS annotation processor, you should clean the output / build directory when you delete or rename the package or class containing a scenario method. The risk is to have a scenario listed to be executed at runtime, which no longer exists.

note.simpara: You can create several methods with the @Scenario annotation in the same class / object.

section.simpara: It is now time to develop the scenario. In the annotated method, create a new scenario and configure it:

callout.para: Creates a new scenario called "hello-world" (see for more details)

Defines the default number of minions to execute, which you can change at runtime (see for more details), they are the number of simulated users or systems that generate load: the more the minions, the more the load
Defines the strategy to start the minion, which you can make faster or slower at runtime (see for more details)

section.title: Specify where the load should be injected
section.simpara: If you consider the scenario as a directed graph joining the steps / actions to execute, you will see that it is possible to have:

listitem.simpara: two branches joining the same step (like a Y)
one step distributing two branches (like a ⅄)

section.simpara: In all those branches, you will have to identify when the load should be injected.

callout.para: The start() statement locates the root of the tree, receiving the load of all the minions at runtime.

section.title: Add steps to your scenario
section.simpara: Now you can add all the steps you want! Let’s go further in our "hello world" scenario.

callout.para: You declare the first step as root of the tree on the scenario, and is generally responsible for generating data to be used in the rest of the tree.
Data can be cached for later use.
Transforms the record.
Retrieves a value previously added to the cache.
Verifies the records using your assertion library of choice, here assertk.
Executes a totally free operation on the step context: you can do whatever you want here, such as printing the "Hello world" message in the console with the start time.
And if you like it, you can repeat it several times with an interval.

section.simpara: Et voila! You’ve just created your first scenario. It is not really useful, but you went through the most important concepts of creating a scenario.
Now you are ready to go further in the documentation and create scenarios that are really relevant for your system.
